VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
Name of Patient/Veteran Patient/Veteran's Social Security Number Date of Examination:
James T. Milner 000-00-0000 08/14/2023
IMPORTANT — THE DEPARTMENT OF VETERANS AFFAIRS (VA) WILL NOT PAY OR REIMBURSE ANY EXPENSES OR COST INCURRED
IN THE PROCESS OF COMPLETING AND/OR SUBMITTING THIS FORM.
Note — The Veteran is applying to the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) for disability benefits. VA will consider the information you provide on this
questionnaire as part of their evaluation in processing the Veteran's claim. It is intended that this questionnaire will be completed by the Veteran's
healthcare provider.
Are you completing this Disability Benefits Questionnaire at the request of:
n Veteran / Claimant
n Third party (list name(s) of organization(s) or individual(s) below)
n Other (please describe below)
Are you a VA Healthcare provider? l Yes n No
Is the Veteran regularly seen as a patient in your clinic? l Yes n No
Was the Veteran examined in person? l Yes n No
EVIDENCE REVIEW
n No records were reviewed
n Records reviewed — Please identify the evidence reviewed and the date range covered:
Evidence identified: Service Treatment Records (STRs) — U.S. Marine Corps, Active Duty 2005–2013 (0311, Rifleman). VA
Treatment Records, Malcom Randall VAMC, Gainesville, FL (2014–2023). VA Mental Health Clinic
records, including psychiatry and individual therapy notes (2014–2023). VA Form 21-526EZ dated
01/09/2023. VA Form 21-0781 (Statement in Support of Claimed Mental Health Disorder Due to
In-Service Traumatic Event) dated 01/15/2023. Buddy statement from SSgt. Kevin Doran, USMC (Ret.),
dated 01/20/2023. Private psychiatric evaluation from Dr. Harold Nguyen, MD, dated 03/04/2023.
SECTION I — DIAGNOSIS
Note: These are condition(s) for which an evaluation has been requested on the exam request form (Internal VA) or for which the Veteran has
requested medical evidence be provided for submission to VA.
1A. List the claimed condition(s) that pertain to this questionnaire:
1. Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) — initial claim 2. Major depressive disorder (MDD) —
claimed as secondary to PTSD 3. Insomnia disorder — claimed as secondary to PTSD
1B. Does the Veteran have a current diagnosis for the claimed condition(s)?
l Yes — provide diagnosis below n No — explain in comments (Section XI)
# Diagnosis (include full name) ICD-10 Code Date of Diagnosis Active / Resolved / Chronic
Posttraumatic stress disorder February
1 F43.10 Chronic — Active
(PTSD), chronic 2014
Major depressive disorder, February
2 F33.1 Chronic — Active
recurrent, moderate 2014
Insomnia disorder, chronic
3 G47.00 March 2014 Chronic — Active
(secondary to PTSD)
Alcohol use disorder, mild (in
4 F10.10 June 2016 In remission since 2019
early remission)
5 — — — —
1C. Is there a secondary or additional diagnosis related to the claimed condition(s)?
l Yes — list below n No

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
Major depressive disorder (F33.1) — secondary to PTSD per DSM-5 comorbidity. Insomnia disorder
(G47.00) — secondary to PTSD, documented by sleep study 04/2021. Alcohol use disorder, mild
(F10.10) — in early remission since 2019; historically used as self-medication for PTSD
hyperarousal and nightmares.
Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ) — PTSD Review Updated on: 2025-04-21 ~v25_1
FICTIONAL SAMPLE — FOR VETERAN PREPARATION / TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT AN OFFICIAL VA DOCUMENT Page 2 of 12

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
SECTION II — MEDICAL HISTORY
2A. Describe the history (including onset, course, and treatment) of the Veteran's condition(s) (brief summary):
James T. Milner is a 38-year-old male, former U.S. Marine Corps Rifleman (MOS 0311), who served on
active duty from March 2005 through October 2013, with three combat deployments to Afghanistan
(Helmand Province, 2007; Helmand Province, 2009–2010; Kandahar Province, 2012). PTSD HISTORY:
Veteran reports onset of PTSD symptoms following multiple severe combat trauma exposures during
his second deployment in 2009–2010. Identified index trauma: Veteran's squad was ambushed on
03/14/2010 near Marjah, Helmand Province. Two Marines from his fire team — LCpl. T. Brooks and
Cpl. D. Santos — were killed by an IED. Veteran reports he held LCpl. Brooks as he died and was
unable to stop the bleeding. This event is corroborated by the buddy statement of SSgt. Kevin
Doran and by USMC unit records confirming the KIA event on that date. Veteran did not seek mental
health treatment while on active duty due to fear of career impact. Post-separation (2013), he
reports escalating nightmares, hypervigilance, avoidance of crowds and loud noises, significant
anger and irritability, and withdrawal from family. He began self-medicating with alcohol in 2015,
which progressed to alcohol use disorder, mild, and entered remission in 2019 with AA and VA
support. First formal PTSD diagnosis made by VA psychiatrist Dr. H. Nguyen in February 2014 at
Malcom Randall VAMC. Currently receiving weekly individual CPT (Cognitive Processing Therapy) with
this examiner and monthly medication management with VA psychiatry. Currently prescribed
sertraline 200mg daily and prazosin 4mg nightly. Veteran reports partial but incomplete response
to medication. Nightmares persist 4–5 nights per week despite prazosin.
2B. In-Service Event or Exposure:
n The condition had its onset during active duty service
n The condition was caused by an in-service event, injury, or exposure (describe below)
n The condition pre-existed service but was aggravated beyond natural progression by service
n No clear relationship to service could be identified
If applicable, describe the in-service event/injury/exposure: Primary index trauma: IED ambush near Marjah, Helmand Province, Afghanistan, 03/14/2010. LCpl. T.
Brooks (KIA) and Cpl. D. Santos (KIA) — both members of Veteran's fire team. Veteran was first
responder and provided direct care to LCpl. Brooks at point of injury. Additional traumas
documented in STRs and Veteran's 21-0781: — Witnessed improvised explosive device detonation
killing 3 civilians, Helmand Province, September 2009. — Sustained mild TBI (concussion) from IED
blast overpressure, October 2009 (STR documented, no LOC). — Multiple direct-fire engagements
across three deployments (2007, 2009–2010, 2012). — Conducted post-blast assessments on multiple
KIA personnel during 2012 deployment.
2C. Does the Veteran's treatment plan include taking continuous medication for the diagnosed
l Yes n No
condition?
If yes, list only those medications used for the diagnosed condition (name, dosage, frequency): 1. Sertraline (Zoloft) 200mg — once daily (PTSD / MDD) 2. Prazosin 4mg — nightly at bedtime
(PTSD-related nightmares / hyperarousal) 3. Hydroxyzine 25mg — as needed for acute anxiety
episodes (max 3x/day) 4. Melatonin 5mg — nightly (insomnia, adjunct to prazosin)
2D. Has the Veteran been hospitalized for this condition within the past 12 months? n Yes l No
No acute inpatient psychiatric hospitalizations in past 12 months. Veteran was seen in VAMC Crisis
Stabilization Unit (outpatient) on 11/08/2022 following a PTSD exacerbation triggered by news
coverage of Afghanistan withdrawal. Released same day with safety plan.
Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ) — PTSD Review Updated on: 2025-04-21 ~v25_1
FICTIONAL SAMPLE — FOR VETERAN PREPARATION / TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT AN OFFICIAL VA DOCUMENT Page 3 of 12

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
SECTION III — CURRENT SYMPTOMS AND SEVERITY
3A. Does the Veteran currently have symptoms attributable to the diagnosed condition(s)? l Yes n No
3B. Symptom Frequency
n Constant (present all
the time) n Daily but intermittent n Several times per week n Weekly or less n Only during flare-ups
3C. Symptom Severity
n Moderate / n Moderately Severe
n None / 0% n Mild / 10–20% 30–50% / 60–70% n Severe / 80–90% n Total / 100%
3D. Symptom Checklist
n Pain (describe location, quality, and intensity) n Dizziness / vertigo / balance problems
n Weakness or loss of strength n Shortness of breath / dyspnea
n Fatigue / lack of endurance n Chest pain
n Limited range of motion n Sleep disturbance / insomnia
n Stiffness or rigidity n Cognitive impairment / memory difficulty
n Swelling / edema / effusion n Anxiety / depression / mood changes
n Numbness or tingling (paresthesia) n Irritability / anger / hypervigilance
n Muscle atrophy or wasting n Flashbacks / intrusive memories
n Tremor or involuntary movement n Social isolation / avoidance
Other symptoms not listed above — describe: Emotional numbing and restricted range of affect. Persistent sense of foreshortened future ('I
don't expect to live to be 60'). Physiological reactivity to cues resembling trauma (helicopters,
fireworks, car backfires) — palpitations, diaphoresis, and acute distress. Dissociative episodes
lasting 1–15 minutes triggered by trauma anniversary dates and unexpected loud noises.
3E. Flare-Ups
Does the Veteran experience flare-ups or periods of acute worsening? l Yes n No
Frequency of flare-ups: n Less than once a month l 1–4 times per month n More than once per week
Duration of flare-ups: n Less than 1 hour n 1–24 hours l 1–7 days n More than 1 week
Describe nature and severity of a typical flare-up, including triggers and recovery time: PTSD flare-ups most commonly triggered by: (1) trauma anniversary dates — especially March 14 each
year (date of Marjah ambush); (2) news coverage of combat or military casualties; (3) unexpected
loud noises (fireworks, backfires, construction); (4) encountering anyone resembling fallen
Marines. During flare-ups, Veteran reports nightly nightmares increasing from 4–5 nights/week to
nightly, severe hypervigilance, social complete withdrawal, inability to leave home, marked
irritability, and in severe cases, passive suicidal ideation without intent or plan. Recovery
typically requires 4–7 days of reduced activity, increased therapy contact, and support from wife.
Veteran denies any current SI at time of this examination.

POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
SECTION IV — PHYSICAL EXAMINATION FINDINGS
4A. General Physical Examination
4A-1. General appearance and Well-groomed male appearing stated age of 38. Casually dressed. Alert
affect: and oriented x3. Affect constricted to restricted range — limited
emotional expressivity during interview. Maintained appropriate eye
contact but frequently scanned the room (hypervigilance behavior
observed). No evidence of psychomotor agitation or retardation.
4A-2. Gait and station: Normal gait and station. Sat in chair closest to the door. Startled
visibly at sound of a door closing in the hallway during the interview.
4A-3. Posture and body habitus: Upright posture. Moderate muscular build. No abnormal posturing.
Appeared tense throughout the interview — shoulders elevated, jaw
clenched at times.
4A-4. Height / Weight / BMI: Height: 5'11" (180.3 cm) | Weight: 196 lbs (88.9 kg) | BMI: 27.3
(Overweight)
4B. Range of Motion: Not applicable to this PTSD examination. No musculoskeletal conditions claimed in this questionnaire.
4C. Neurological Findings
5/5 bilaterally in all tested muscle groups. No focal weakness
Muscle strength:
detected.
Sensation: Grossly intact to light touch throughout. No reported paresthesias.
Deep tendon reflexes: 2+ bilaterally and symmetric. No pathological reflexes.
Coordination: Finger-nose intact. No ataxia. Romberg negative.
Gait abnormalities / ataxia: None. Gait normal.
4D. Mental Health Clinical Observations
Mental Status Examination — check all that apply:
n Alert and oriented × 3 (person, place, time) n Disorganized or tangential thought process
n Cooperative and forthcoming during interview n Impaired memory or concentration observed
n Anxious or guarded demeanor n Suicidal ideation — passive
n Depressed or flat affect n Suicidal ideation — active, with plan
n Tearful during interview n Homicidal ideation
n Agitated or irritable behavior n Hallucinations / psychotic features observed
PTSD / Trauma Symptom Domains — check all reported or observed:
Re-experiencing:
n Intrusive memories / recollections
n Recurrent distressing nightmares
n Dissociative flashback episodes
n Intense psychological distress at cues
n Physiological reactions to trauma cues
Avoidance:
n Avoidance of trauma-related thoughts or feelings
n Avoidance of external reminders (people, places, activities)
Negative Alterations in Cognition & Mood:

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
n Inability to remember key aspects of trauma
n Persistent negative beliefs about self or world
n Distorted blame of self or others
n Persistent negative emotional state
n Markedly diminished interest in activities
n Feelings of detachment or estrangement
n Inability to experience positive emotions
Arousal & Reactivity:
n Irritability / angry outbursts
n Reckless or self-destructive behavior
n Hypervigilance
n Exaggerated startle response
n Difficulty concentrating
n Sleep disturbance
Examiner's narrative observations on mental status and PTSD symptom presentation: Mr. Milner presented as cooperative but guarded. He required several minutes to compose himself
before discussing the primary trauma event. He became visibly tearful when describing the death of
LCpl. Brooks, stating: 'I keep seeing his face every time I close my eyes.' He demonstrated clear
hypervigilance behaviors throughout the interview (door-scanning, startling, rigid posture).
Thought content was logical and goal-directed. No evidence of psychosis or formal thought
disorder. Memory intact for remote events; mild deficits in short-term recall noted (scored 4/5 on
delayed recall task). Insight is good — Veteran acknowledges the impact of PTSD on his
functioning. Judgment intact. Veteran denied any current suicidal ideation, plan, or intent.
Safety plan is current and on file. Wife is identified support person.
4E. Cardiorespiratory Findings
Blood pressure (R arm / L arm): R: 142/90 mmHg / L: 140/88 mmHg (mildly elevated — noted)
92 bpm, regular rate and rhythm (elevated likely anxiety-related
Heart rate and rhythm:
during exam)
Respiratory rate / On saturation: 18 breaths/min / On Sat: 99% on room air
Chest auscultation findings: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No adventitious sounds.
Not formally assessed. Veteran reports reduced exercise tolerance
METs capacity / exercise tolerance: secondary to motivational deficits from MDD and social avoidance from
PTSD. Deconditioned by history.
4F. Dermatological / Scars
Does the Veteran have scars or skin conditions related to the claimed condition? n Yes l No
No scars or skin conditions related to PTSD or MDD at this time.
Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ) — PTSD Review Updated on: 2025-04-21 ~v25_1

POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
SECTION V — SURGICAL TREATMENT
5A. Has the Veteran had surgical treatment for any claimed condition? n Yes l No
No surgical treatment for PTSD, MDD, or insomnia.
5B. Does the Veteran have any residuals or complications as a result of surgery? n Yes l No
Not applicable — no surgery performed.
Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ) — PTSD Review Updated on: 2025-04-21 ~v25_1
FICTIONAL SAMPLE — FOR VETERAN PREPARATION / TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT AN OFFICIAL VA DOCUMENT Page 7 of 12

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
SECTION VI — FUNCTIONAL IMPACT
6A. Occupational Impact
Current employment status: n Employed full-time n Employed part-time n Unemployed due to condition n Retired n Other
6A-1. Do the Veteran's conditions impact his or her ability to work? l Yes n No
If yes, describe impact of each condition, providing one or more examples: PTSD: Veteran reports he was forced to leave his full-time position as a construction foreman in
2019 due to inability to tolerate noise on job sites (jackhammers, nail guns) and difficulty
managing a crew due to anger outbursts. He currently works 20 hours/week as a night-shift
inventory clerk at a big-box retailer — a role he selected specifically because it minimizes human
contact. He reports frequent call-outs during PTSD flare-ups (estimated 3–4 days/month missed).
His employer has verbally warned him about attendance. MDD: Veteran reports mornings are most
difficult — severe anhedonia and fatigue make it hard to get out of bed. He has missed multiple
scheduled shifts due to inability to function in the morning hours. Insomnia: Veteran averages 3–4
hours of fragmented sleep per night despite medication. This contributes directly to daytime
cognitive impairment and fatigue affecting work.
6B. Individual Unemployability (TDIU)
6B-1. Do the Veteran's service-connected disabilities prevent him or her from securing or following a
l Yes n No
substantially gainful occupation?
If yes, describe how the disabilities, taken together, preclude substantially gainful employment: The combination of chronic PTSD (hypervigilance, hyperarousal, avoidance, nightmares, flashbacks),
comorbid MDD (anhedonia, fatigue, reduced concentration), and chronic insomnia render Mr. Milner
unable to sustain full-time substantially gainful employment. His current part-time, low-contact,
night-shift role represents the maximum employment this Veteran can maintain. Examiner's clinical
opinion is that this Veteran meets the criteria for TDIU consideration. Referral to VA Form
21-8940 is strongly recommended.
6C. Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
Moderate
Activity No Limitation Mild Limitation Severe Limitation Unable to Perform
Limitation
Bathing / personal hygiene n l n n n
Dressing / grooming n l n n n
Meal preparation n n l n n
Household chores n n l n n
Driving / transportation n l n n n
Shopping / errands n n n l n
Climbing stairs l n n n n
Walking (> 1 block) l n n n n
Exercise / recreation n n n l n
Sexual activity n n l n n
Sleep / rest n n n l n
Social interaction n n n l n
6D. Occupational and Social Impairment Level (Mental Health — check one)
n No occupational or social impairment
n Mild — occasional decrease in efficiency; generally functions satisfactorily
n Moderate — reduced reliability and productivity; some difficulty in social, occupational, or school functioning
Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ) — PTSD Review Updated on: 2025-04-21 ~v25_1
FICTIONAL SAMPLE — FOR VETERAN PREPARATION / TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT AN OFFICIAL VA DOCUMENT Page 8 of 12

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
l Moderate-to-Severe — deficiencies in most areas such as work, school, family relations, judgment, thinking, or mood
n Severe — persistent danger of hurting self or others, or inability to perform ADLs, or gross impairment in communication
n Total — persistent inability to perform basic self-care activities; requiring continuous hospitalization
Rationale for impairment level selected: Moderate-to-Severe selected based on: deficiencies in occupational functioning (cannot maintain
full-time work, multiple missed shifts/month); deficiencies in family relations (wife reports
significant strain, limited intimacy, Veteran's emotional unavailability); deficiencies in
judgment during PTSD flare-ups (impulsive anger reported); and persistent mood deficits from
comorbid MDD. Does not yet meet Severe as Veteran is not in persistent danger of harm to self or
others and is able to maintain basic self-care.
6E. Assistive Devices
n Cane / walking stick n Crutches n Walker / rollator n Manual wheelchair
n Power wheelchair / scooter n Brace / splint / orthotic n CPAP / BiPAP machine n Hearing aids
n Prosthetic limb n Oxygen supplementation n None
Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ) — PTSD Review Updated on: 2025-04-21 ~v25_1

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
SECTION VII — DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
7A. Have diagnostic imaging studies or other diagnostic procedures been performed? l Yes n No
If yes, check all that apply and provide results:
n X-ray (plain radiograph) Date: — — Not applicable to PTSD claim.
n MRI Date: — — Not indicated for this evaluation.
n CT scan Date: — — Not indicated.
n Ultrasound Date: — — Not applicable.
n EKG/ECG Date: — — Not performed.
n Pulmonary function tests Date: — — Not indicated.
n Audiogram Date: — — Not claimed in this DBQ.
n ENG Date: — — Not applicable.
n Nerve conduction study / EMG Date: — — Not applicable.
n Laboratory work (blood/urine) Date: — — Not performed at this examination.
n Neuropsychological testing Date: 04/18/2023 CAPS-5 / CAPS-5 total score: 68 (Severe PTSD). PCL-5:
PCL-5 / PHQ-9 / BDI-II 61/80 (severe). PHQ-9: 17 (Moderately Severe
depression). BDI-II: 28 (Moderate-Severe).
All scores consistent with chronic, severe
PTSD with comorbid MDD.
n Other: Sleep Study (PSG) Date: 04/21/2021 Confirmed insomnia disorder. Sleep efficiency
Polysomnography 54% (normal >85%). Mean sleep onset latency
48 min. Multiple awakenings. No apnea events.
REM behavior findings consistent with
PTSD-related nightmare disorder.
7B. Are there any other significant diagnostic test findings and/or results? l Yes n No
If yes, provide type of test or procedure, date, and results (brief summary): PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist for DSM-5) administered at intake and every 90 days per VAMC PTSD protocol.
Scores over past 12 months range from 56–64, consistent with severe, chronic PTSD without
significant improvement trend despite treatment. PHQ-9 (depression screen) consistently ‡15 over
past 12 months indicating moderately severe depression. GAD-7 (anxiety screen): 16/21 at last
assessment (severe anxiety range). Audit-C alcohol screening: 2/12 — consistent with current
abstinence / early remission.
Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ) — PTSD Review Updated on: 2025-04-21 ~v25_1
FICTIONAL SAMPLE — FOR VETERAN PREPARATION / TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT AN OFFICIAL VA DOCUMENT Page 10 of 12

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
SECTION VIII — NEXUS OPINION (SERVICE CONNECTION)
Note: This section documents the examiner's medical opinion on whether the claimed condition is related to military service. The legal standard is
'at least as likely as not' (50% or greater probability). The opinion must be accompanied by a supporting rationale.
8A. Type of Service Connection
l Direct Service Connection — condition began in or was directly caused by active duty service
n Aggravation — pre-existing condition was permanently worsened beyond its natural progression by service
n Secondary Service Connection — condition was caused or aggravated by an already service-connected disability
n Presumptive Service Connection — condition is presumed related to service based on era, location, or exposure
n 38 U.S.C. § 1151 — condition was caused or aggravated by VA medical treatment or vocational rehabilitation
8B. Examiner's Medical Opinion
l AT LEAST AS LIKELY AS NOT (50% or greater probability) — SERVICE CONNECTION SUPPORTED
n LESS LIKELY AS NOT (less than 50% probability) — SERVICE CONNECTION NOT SUPPORTED
n CANNOT BE DETERMINED WITHOUT ADDITIONAL INFORMATION — DEFERRED
8C. Rationale — Provide the reasoning and evidence supporting the opinion above: PTSD (F43.10): It is at least as likely as not (50% or greater probability) that the Veteran's
chronic PTSD is directly caused by his military service in the U.S. Marine Corps, specifically by
combat trauma exposures sustained during three deployments to Afghanistan (2007, 2009–2010, 2012).
The following evidence supports this opinion: 1. CONFIRMED IN-SERVICE STRESSOR: The primary index
trauma — the IED ambush on 03/14/2010 near Marjah, Helmand Province resulting in the combat deaths
of LCpl. T. Brooks and Cpl. D. Santos — is corroborated by: (a) USMC unit records confirming the
KIA event on that date; (b) buddy statement of SSgt. Kevin Doran; and (c) the Veteran's detailed,
consistent, and emotionally congruent account across multiple clinical contacts spanning 9 years.
2. DSM-5 DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA MET: The Veteran fully meets DSM-5 Criterion A (direct exposure to
actual death of others in a combat context) and all symptom clusters: B (re-experiencing), C
(avoidance), D (negative alterations in cognition and mood), and E (alterations in arousal and
reactivity). Duration exceeds one month (Criterion F). Functional impairment is documented
(Criterion G). Symptoms are not attributable to substances or another medical condition (Criterion
H). 3. CHRONOLOGICAL CONSISTENCY: Onset of symptoms is documented in VAMC records beginning
February 2014, approximately 4 months post-separation — consistent with delayed-expression PTSD
presentation. No evidence of pre-service psychiatric history. 4. SEVERITY: CAPS-5 score of 68
falls in the severe range. PCL-5 score of 61 corroborates severity. Inadequate response to two
first-line treatments (sertraline and CPT) supports chronicity and severity of service-related
PTSD. SECONDARY CONDITIONS: MDD (F33.1) and insomnia disorder (G47.00) are at least as likely as
not secondary to PTSD, as both are well-documented comorbidities of chronic combat PTSD and both
emerged concurrently with the PTSD diagnosis in 2014. No pre-service history of depression or
insomnia is documented.
Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ) — PTSD Review Updated on: 2025-04-21 ~v25_1
FICTIONAL SAMPLE — FOR VETERAN PREPARATION / TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT AN OFFICIAL VA DOCUMENT Page 11 of 12

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
SECTION IX — OTHER PERTINENT PHYSICAL FINDINGS
9A. Does the Veteran have any other pertinent physical findings, complications, conditions, signs or
l Yes n No
symptoms related to the conditions listed in the diagnosis section above?
If yes, describe (brief summary): Mildly elevated blood pressure (142/90) noted during examination — likely anxiety-related and
consistent with sympathetic arousal; however, chronic hypertension secondary to PTSD-related
chronic stress response cannot be excluded. Recommend follow-up with primary care for hypertension
evaluation. Alcohol use disorder (F10.10), mild, in early remission since 2019 — historically used
as self-medication for PTSD hyperarousal and nightmares. While in remission, the disorder itself
is a consequence of the PTSD and may warrant separate claim consideration as secondary to PTSD.
9B. Does the Veteran have any scars or other disfigurement related to any conditions or treatment listed
n Yes l No
in the diagnosis section?
9C. Comments, if any: Mr. Milner presented with a well-documented and clinically compelling PTSD case. His in-service
stressors are confirmed by independent corroborating evidence. His symptom trajectory and
treatment response are consistent with chronic, severe combat-related PTSD. He has engaged
consistently with VA mental health treatment for nine years, demonstrating good faith effort at
rehabilitation. This examiner strongly recommends: (1) TDIU referral via VA Form 21-8940; (2)
evaluation of secondary conditions MDD and insomnia for separate service connection; (3)
evaluation for sleep apnea as secondary to PTSD (not addressed in this DBQ but noted clinically);
(4) continued weekly CPT and monthly medication management; (5) peer support specialist referral.
Veteran expressed willingness to continue treatment and was in agreement with examination
findings. Safety plan reviewed and updated. No acute safety concerns at time of discharge from
examination.
Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ) — PTSD Review Updated on: 2025-04-21 ~v25_1
FICTIONAL SAMPLE — FOR VETERAN PREPARATION / TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT AN OFFICIAL VA DOCUMENT Page 12 of 12

VA Department of Veterans Affairs DISABILITY BENEFITS QUESTIONNAIRE (DBQ)
POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) — REVIEW
SECTION X — TUMORS AND NEOPLASMS (Complete only if applicable)
10A. Does the Veteran have a benign or malignant neoplasm or metastases related to any of the
n Yes l No
diagnoses in Section I?
Not applicable. No neoplasms identified or claimed.
SECTION XI — REMARKS
11A. Remarks (if any): Examination conducted at Malcom Randall VAMC PTSD Specialty Clinic, Gainesville, FL on 08/14/2023.
Examination duration: 2 hours 20 minutes. This examiner has provided ongoing weekly CPT to Mr.
Milner since March 2022 and has reviewed all available VAMC mental health records spanning
2014–2023. VA Form 21-0781 (Statement in Support of Claimed Mental Health Disorder) reviewed and
found consistent with clinical presentation and documented VAMC records. Buddy statement from
SSgt. Kevin Doran reviewed and corroborates the primary in-service stressor event of 03/14/2010.
Private psychiatric evaluation by Dr. Harold Nguyen, MD (03/04/2023) also reviewed — findings
consistent with this examiner's evaluation.
SECTION XII — EXAMINER'S CERTIFICATION AND SIGNATURE
CERTIFICATION — To the best of my knowledge, the information contained herein is accurate, complete and current.
PENALTY: The law provides severe penalties which include fine or imprisonment, or both, for the willful submission of any statement or evidence

decision_letter.text:
DEPARTMENT OF VETERANS AFFAIRS
Veterans Benefits Administration — Rating Decision
Veteran: James T. Milner
VA File Number: 000-00-0000
Decision Date: 11/15/2023
INTRODUCTION
We reviewed your claim along with all submitted DBQs and C&P; examination results.
DECISION
1. Service connection for Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) — GRANTED.
2. Service connection for Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) — DENIED.
3. Service connection for Ear Condition (Tinnitus / Vestibular Disorder) — DENIED.
EVIDENCE
- ALS DBQ confirming diagnosis and severity nfilecitenturn1file0n
- PTSD DBQ showing symptoms but insufficient nexus nfilecitenturn1file2n
- Ear Conditions DBQ with inconsistent findings nfilecitenturn1file1n
- General C&P; Examination report nfilecitenturn1file3n
REASONS FOR DECISION
ALS: Granted due to presumptive service connection under federal regulation.
PTSD: Denied due to lack of sufficient evidence linking condition directly to service.
Ear Condition: Denied due to insufficient medical nexus and lack of consistent documentation.
